transcript.text: Oh, hello.
Hey, Matthew.
How's it going?
Hey, how are you doing?
Yeah, I'm not bad, thank you.
Good.
Wonderful, wonderful.
Yes, so my name is Matthew.
I'm the chief content officer here at GrowthX.
That's here a little bit different here than it is elsewhere.
I do some external work, but mostly internal work, kind of deciding what good looks like across the content quality that we deliver to clients and how to articulate improvements to that.
Into our platform internally that we use to generate content and to create output for our clients.
And then a little bit of team building work and process oriented work.
So a little bit different than a typical content role, but the same as many content roles.
And then I care mostly about content quality.
So that's what I do here.
And pleased to meet you and happy to hear a little bit about you.
Yeah, so my name is John Anthony.
I am currently...
Senior Tech Writer for Tech Writer, where I cover all things consumer AI, so like AI and consumer technology.
I was previously an editor at iMore, which was like an Apple-centric news website, also under the future publishing company brand.
And, yeah, I live in Scotland, in Edinburgh, and I, yeah, I'm, yeah, I hate intros because I'm terrible at them.
That's good enough, that's good enough.
I'm very familiar with iMore, Renee, Ritchie, and I go way back, so definitely familiar with the earlier days of it, especially.
Okay, cool.
Let's, let's talk about a few things.
So one, like, big thing that, I mean, the role here is...
A lot of it is process-oriented, right?
So we expect, obviously, anybody coming in to have a good and a solid understanding of what good content feels like, the textural, the mouthfeel of good quality, what that looks and feels like.
So that's sort of hip pocket.
The new stuff or the interesting we-don't-know bits about people from their resumes is the kind of process-oriented stuff.
Because a lot of the process, a lot of the work we do in here internally is oriented around the process of content creation because the writers themselves are our workflows internally.
And so managing those workflows and understanding their outputs and inputs has a lot to do with the process of writing and editing and articulation.
So let's talk about a couple of things related to that.
I think one primary one is editorial judgment.
So how do you personally judge whether a piece of content-
Is high quality, like what's your process for understanding, hey, this feels good, feels sound, it's a good piece of content?
I think it's really important to kind of have awareness of the context in which the content is going to exist, right?
So a really important thing for me when I'm editing an article is to have wider context of what the writer is kind of writing about.
And then being able to kind of like make sure that if somebody was going to read this with a critical eye, they would not be able to pull it apart.
So it's a frustration that I have in my current role and I have, so I've been at, like to be totally candid with you, I've been a tech writer for just over a year now.
And when I was at iMore, I was in an editorial role where I dealt a lot with freelancers and I built...
Basically a how-to section around all things Apple.

And before iMore, I'd been in an editorial role and I've always had that kind of focus on thinking about what makes good content and liking to direct the kind of direction of the content to kind of fit whether that's based off of SEO and trying to like target keywords.
Or if that's just based off of what's kind of relevant in the tech sphere and trying to target kind of what I think people are interested in.
So iMore is no longer a functioning website.
So Future basically closed the website.
But just before it closed, I saw an opportunity to move to TechRadar and it was about AI and Apple had just announced Apple Intelligence.
and I was feeling like it was a really interesting opportunity because it would have...
allowed me to kind of be at the forefront of talking about AI and consumer tech.
And this was still at a point where AI was definitely very popular, but it wasn't quite like my parents didn't talk about it yet.
you know what I mean?
So I felt like there was an opportunity there to really be at the forefront of that.
And I like to take risks in that sense.
So I moved over to TechRadar.
But by moving over to TechRadar, went from an editorial role where I had full control over section to a senior writer under a managing editor or under a senior editor.
And so whilst in TechRadar, I'm still technically like so the people that I work with daily will treat me like an editor, right?
So I have a lot of say in the content that gets done for the section.
I speak up a lot in calls.
We have a lot of chat.
I have a real issue.
With the wider team, because the wider team don't know me as an editor.
They know me as a writer who writes about AI.

And I was, I said in the previous call, when you're writing about AI for an AI section on a website that is traditionally all about consumer technology with journalists that feel threatened by AI, you're viewed as the enemy internally, right?
So I, I, I will pick up on articles throughout TechRadar and I will point them out.

And, and that's because I think that there's, there's often, going back to your initial question, there's often kind of that, with something like TechRadar, which is quite widespread, sometimes articles slip through the cracks, which don't read as if they're written by an expert.
And, and, and so I think it's.
Really critical in the editorial process that regardless of what you're writing about, it's better to not go in depth than go in depth and be wrong.
you know what I mean?
that's kind of my editorial eye.
It's like thinking about the wider context of the world and thinking about where the piece should fit and if it should fit and then deciding, okay, cool, it has a place.
You know, let's make sure that it's surefire.
You know what I mean?
Yes.
Yeah, it makes sense.
And then like, I guess the dovetail with that is something that we deal with a lot with client work because they have deadlines.
Like journalists are familiar, you know, you've got to get the piece out at some point.
And what is your rubric, your personal rubric for deciding something is good enough?
So I used to be a massive perfectionist.
And then when I was studying journalism at university, my, uh, my...
The journalism professor told me, done is better than perfect.
And that stuck with me throughout my whole career, where I think you can always edit and make something better.
Like, especially in, like you said, when you're working for an online website, or working in online journalism, or even with print where there's a deadline.
Like, you're always kind of trying to meet a deadline.
So, like, there's always going to be elements that can be tweaked.

I think, again, it goes back to that point of, like, as long as you are aware that you, as long as the content doesn't veer to the point where it feels like somebody isn't literate about what they're talking about.
And you know, you need to, the content needs to read like it.
It's written by somebody who knows what they're talking about.
And if that can come out the page, then as far as I'm concerned, the piece will be all right, regardless of whether it could be 200 words longer and better.
So, yeah, it's a funny one.
I feel like I, and it sounds cliche, I'm not the best at vocalizing, right?
I ramble.
I'm from an Italian family, like I'm loud and I ramble.
But I know, but I've always had the skill of being able to look at words and know if something flows correctly and if it sounds good.
And that's something that has been with me since I've been, since I started studying journalism and it stuck with me.
And that's why I think in terms of when I have had C and kind of my hands on the editorial.
came to tutorial.
Process.
Like you can definitely, I can, I definitely see the results from that.
And so do other people.
Yeah.
That's good.
I mean, the, you know, the reason I asked obviously is because a good amount of the role is articulating the difference between what you wish was happening and what is happening.
Right.

Because we, when we architect the pipelines and they run, there is always, because nothing's ever perfect, there's always a delta between the outcome of any individual step or the pipeline as a whole and what you would like to happen.

So the articulation that you're talking about there, we're not, we don't need it to be in spoken word format, but written word format is vital because we need to be able to parse and understand, hey, what is it?
Okay.
You said this is bad.
How precisely?
And what would make it good?
Right.
In ways, of course, that LLMs can understand.

But also that human engineers can understand because the pipelines really are affected by kind of three major things, prompt engineering or the instructions that you give them, the semantics involved, context engineering, you know, what context you give them in which to work so that they can understand the subjects, the topicals, matter that you're working with, the audiences and the clients that you're working with.
And then engineering, right?
Which is not your job, but it is your job to say, hey, I have examined the other aspects of this.
And I feel that the instructions are clear, that the context is rich, but it is still not performing the task appropriately or adequately.
And that's when we're like, cool, fire up a ticket, let's write a description and a detailed articulation of the Delta, and let's have the engineering team tackle it.
So that's why process.

That's an articulation of that process is important to us, which kind of leads me to my next question, which is, I would love to hear, and obviously this would be more applicable to your previous role as an editor or section leader, but kind of describe a content or editorial process that you saw that was existent or even non-existent, and you either established it or improved it.
I want like a pragmatic example of like, hey, this was happening, it caused X issues, and here's how I went about creating a process for that.

So the reason why I more hired a how-to editor, because you didn't have a how-to editor at the time, was because they were trying to focus on evergreen content, and they needed structure, and they needed kind of, with a new editor-in-chief, they needed a real strategy with the way that the website targeted kind of guides for people, right?
I, so...
When I came in, there was no style guide, there was no kind of freelancers that were working on that kind of stuff.
And there was kind of a misunderstanding about what good how-to content could be.
So I found, and it was a battle at the start, but I found that when you say how-to guide or how-to article, people just think it has to be kind of like incredibly dry and incredibly process-based.
And that's not to say that they can't be, because sometimes you want things to be dry and process-based and tell you exactly what you need to do.
But I really wanted to make the content specifically in how-to's stand out as kind of like...
Because I was, so when I was at university, I worked at Apple, in the Apple store, and I was at the Genius Bar.
And...
Yeah.

And from there, I took a lot of stuff about how I really wanted to give people the tools and then showcase them how they could use those tools to pursue whatever passion they wanted to do or whatever they were trying to achieve.
And when I started that role, it was all important about how do you make that into a how-to article.
So I started with a specific iMore how-to style guide and then basically kind of showcased to the wider iMore team how a how-to doesn't just need to be step-by-step.
And then working with freelancers, they essentially were showing, so when starting out, I did a couple of like, as I was getting into the role.
More articles were starting up here, which kind of showed the direction of it, but I was able to like showcase, okay, this is what we're, this is what we're wanting to do.
This is how I would do it.

And this is the guide of what I'd like you to follow and work with them in terms of deciding how the best way to, to position specific kind of boring keyword kind of set targeting, how we could make that, how we could focus on that.
But then, okay, I want you to do this, but I want you to think about the wider picture.

And so I, my editorial process was always working quite hands-on with the freelancers to make it so that the pieces could really shine and not just be kind of checking a box or a box checking exercise, really.
And so, yeah, that, by the end, by the end of my time at Highmore, there was, there was a real structure to the, to the how-tos and to the section as a whole.
So, yeah.
And I felt like we kind of achieved that.
In my current role at TechRadar, like, so in February, we had like an ad campaign.
we had like extra money that we needed to put into the section for freelancing.
So I got basically given the role of freelancing and commissioning out 40 articles for the AI section in February.
And my process with that was to really focus on, I basically was like, I needed to look at what was doing well in terms of Google Discover.
And we'd seen a couple of trends related to the way that if we were to focus on things like ChatGPT, primarily ChatGPT, and really kind of spin it so that it was all about.
And people's personal interaction with AI and what they'd learned from it, then when we did that, we were seeing better results.
So I decided that I had 40 articles, so I would really hammer home that point.
And all my freelancers did very specific stuff around that.
And the numbers in terms of the AI section are February has been our most successful of the year.
And as soon as, yeah, so.
That's great.
Yeah.
Yeah, thank you.

And then I think that's, you my third big question is, and like, I'm going to come at this with some context, which I wouldn't normally address with just anybody, but given that you're in journalism, I want you to know that, like, I was in journalism for 15 years.
And I know what people in journalism feel about AI and AI-generated content.
So given that context, I have to know, like, how much have you utilized AI and LLM tools in generating content, editing content, or creating processes for research or things like that?
Talk to me about some pragmatic examples that you have done and utilized there.
I'd love to hear about it.
And this is a judgment-free zone because we are completely agnostic about it and utilize it every day.
So you don't have to couch it in any sort of reservation about AR or LLMs.
It is a sort of free radical force of nature, and we're utilizing it.
That's the way we look at it.
So I'd love to hear about the specific examples or ways that you've used it.
So I have Notion set up with ChatGB.

To basically do a scan of the trending topics of the world of AI and specific to consumer technology every morning, which is a really useful tool because it saves me a lot of time having to kind of look through pages and pages of news stories to find kind of specifics.
I also use ChatGPT for like headline help.
So I'll kind of use it more as like a conversational chatbot to like discuss headlines and come up with ideas.
Future is very strict on the use of AI.
So there's a lot of stuff that's not necessarily allowed.
So like in terms of using it for content, that's like a big no-no.
for content, can can you can You

I would be lying if I said I haven't experimented, not for things that have gone public, but I have experimented with basically prompt engineering and ChatGPT and Gemini and Claude to see how far I can push it in terms of using my portfolio articles to get AI to write like me.

And then shaping the way that it comes back to basically tailor it so that if I wanted to, I think I can do a pretty good job at getting a ChatBot to basically write an article that I would have to edit slightly and then it would be in my tone of voice and sound like me and I could give it to my wife and she could read it and probably wouldn't be able to tell.
So yeah, it's a hard one because in terms of in the workplace, I'm...
I'm very limited.
I use AI because I have to use AI to then write, but I'm not using AI to write or help.
Right, right.
Yes, if that makes sense.
Yeah, and that's totally fine.
mean, the way that I look at it, I am personally agnostic about whether people are using it for work or personally.
I don't care.

What matters to us is familiarity with the systems, understanding the inputs and outputs, how semantics and context engineering, the context that you give it, how the systems interact with one another, like all of that matters to us.
How you garner that knowledge and how you achieve it is, I don't care, right?
Like if it's personal work where you're like, hey, I cannot stop working with it, so much the better, right?
Like, and you have professional limitations, which I understand, right?
Like if you have an editorial policy against it, okay, cool.
Yeah, you know, it should be, right?
Or if you don't, then it should be transparent.
Like the worst case scenario is...
You have an editorial policy that allows it, but then the organization is not transparent about it to the reader, right?
So that, I think, is very important in journalism, right?
But from a personal level, it seems like you have been experimenting with it.
And are you creating other workflows for yourself?
Like you mentioned, the notion integration, which I think is a great way to go.
So I know when, I'm dating myself, but like 2009, 2010, I built a Yahoo Pipes system that did much the similar.
Yahoo Pipes was a precursor to IFTTT or Zapier, I guess, would be like the modern analogy.
But basically a Yahoo Pipes to grab dozens of RSS feeds and surface content that was relevant to my coverage areas.
Largely Apple at the time, but other coverage areas too, that allowed me to kind of monitor the trends.
And then a series of page monitors to ping when company...
Blogs or technical pages were updated.
And so we basically created like a master feed that we then piped into the Twitter API.
And then we had a private Twitter account that we had that fed us all that information.
And that's how we were able to compete with very large news organizations.
And they were always like astounded at how fast we were, even though it was like nine people.
And, you know, we're competing with the times and beating them on stories, like 1500 person newsroom.
And it's like, yeah, because you're not hungry, right?
You're lazy, you're fat and lazy, metaphorically speaking.
You know, that I think was, that's kind of what we're getting at here is like, I don't hear how you come by the skills.
Are you interested in it?
Have you experimented with it?
Do you have familiarity?
Because I will be honest, you will be up to your eyeballs immediately.
You know, we are working at the edge of what is currently possible with LLMs.
And- Alton.
All
Alternately, on a daily basis, sometimes intraday, succeeding and failing.
That's the job of a startup, right?
It's like, hey, work at the edge of what is possible and step over the ledge until the bricks appear under your feet and hope that they will.
And that's kind of what I'm getting at there.
So if there's any additional kind of ways that you're using LLMs or AI and you want to articulate that, happy to hear any more.
Well, I experiment with new AI software all the time because, basically, I'll get early access to stuff and use it and then write about it.
So, like, at the moment, I've been experimenting a lot with, like, AI web browsers.
So, like, for example, last week I was using Opera Neon to basically create my own bookmarks by basically getting it to, like, vibe code.
And create specific web pages for things that I wanted to, like, track.
So, like, I had one...
Or like RSS feeds of like the specific sections on TechRadar that I was interested in.
Or like tracking like Metacritic ratings for like video games, just like a Fathom project and things.
But yeah, I play around with a lot of stuff in terms of like writing.
That's probably as far as it goes, what I previously said.
But yeah, I use a lot of, I try to use AI from the perspective of how I think a reader would use AI.
So I think in that sense, it limits what I potentially do with it.
But that's more of a choice coming at it from a journalist who writes about AI, if that makes sense.
Yeah, that makes sense.
Yeah, great.
I'm happy to answer any questions you may have about kind of the way we work or to dive in on anything there.
Articulate any of our processes, that sort of thing.
Yeah.
I guess.

I'm interested to know, because I know you were at TechCrunch for a long time, how are you enjoying the role that you're in, and how do you feel about being on the other side of the world of journalism in the sense of you're now not really working in the same field, but you kind of are, but you're not, but you are.

Yeah, no, like I spent, you know, 15 years covering startups and people building all kinds of really incredible stuff, and I got into it very explicitly because, not because, I wanted to be Walter Cronkite.
That was not my goal.
My goal was, technology is fascinating.
I am endlessly obsessed with how these things get created, hardware to software, the integration of both, how teams work together to create stuff.
I am a neophyte coder, so I can...
Write code, but largely these days, who writes code?
I use codex and cloud code mostly to code projects or tools that I want.
But I was endlessly fascinated with that world and sort of finally was able to get to a point where I'm like, hey, I accomplished pretty much what I need to accomplish in the world of journalism.
I'm editor-in-chief of one of the largest tech publications in the world for like 10 years.
I don't really need to go be an EISC of a different publication that's probably more boring or maybe pay more but be more bureaucratic than I care to because we honestly operated on our own universe.
mean, publications like Tech Grounds will not exist in the future.
It's just impossible.
It's not going to happen.
Those are mostly solo apps now.
The TV PMs of the world or the solo newsletter entrepreneurs of the world, those folks are charting new courses, which is great.
I'm happy for them, you know?
I don't
But newsrooms like TechCrunch won't exist again.
So I had reached essentially the pinnacle of that universe and had always been deeply obsessed with how product gets made and just wanted to go work in products.
So that's why I'm here.
So a large portion of my day is talking with engineers, filing tickets, massaging things, utilizing the product at a deep level.
I run a few clients because I want to get a really close read on how our tools are actually operating for clients.
I'll hand those off eventually when I get bored, but not bored, but like I got other things to do, but it's important, right?

Like I don't want to go to an ME, you know, who's tasked with operating these pipelines to deliver high quality content work for a client and give them feedback or talk to them about why they're having difficulties or articulate it without understanding the process intimately.
So I do that and then help build the process and iterate.
And that makes me extremely happy.
So I'm making less money now.
And I did after 10 years in a corporation, as you can imagine, but I'm happier for it.
So that's where I'm at.
That's cool.
So in terms of the content pipeline at the moment, what would you say is the biggest kind of friction or bottleneck at the moment?
So what's your biggest challenge in terms of the content pipeline just now?
I think it varies from client to client.
There are a couple of major work streams that we operate for the vast majority of clients.

We consider editorial, that whole bucket of things that would be like organic editorial content, not paid media, not strictly peer marketing, more organic content, supporting content, as you can imagine, how-tos are part of that.
we're find out more.
you.

Lists, comparisons, fundamental articles of interest to an ICP of a client, to their audience they want to attract.
That's probably one of our major work streams.
And then we have other work streams like programmatic SEO, which would be more like, hey, as an example, you probably wrote some guides at some point, right?
If I want to spin up a guide site for a client, let's say Samsung came to us and they're like, hey, we really want like a guide on how to use Samsung phones for photography.
I could probably spec out that plan within a day, build a pipeline within a couple of days and probably have them 200 pages generated within a week.
We don't do that at that scale because there's a lot of stuff that needs to happen in between, right?
You need design and engineering and, you
You deployment and all that stuff, but I could reliably ship a dozen pages every week for months on end for them using the pipelines.
So they're really good at that sort of programmatic work.
In the editorial world, it requires a little bit more sophistication depending on the client.
So as an example, we have a client that is a women's supplement company.
Um, they offer supplements that include maybe a dozen different ingredients, all natural ingredients, but many of them trademarked and they, each ingredient, um, attacks a specific symptom.
Um, they're mostly focused on perimenopausal women, postmenopausal women, and reproductive era women.
Um, that requires some specificity when you're dealing with it, generating an article.
Um, there are rules like, Hey, we want these ingredients to be mentioned in the context in which they
So as an example, let's write an article about saffron because that's one of the ingredients in the product.
Saffron addresses inflammation.
It addresses various other conditions that are present in perimenopause, during perimenopause, or maybe exacerbated during perimenopause.
So when we mention it, we mention saffron as an ingredient.
It's included in the product.
So let's talk about it.
But when it's mentioned in context with the product, we actually need to mention that it's saffron, saffron's trademarks.
They paid for the clinical upgrade.
They paid for the brand name.
So that must be mentioned alongside it.
This, as you can imagine, is similar to any editorial checklist you might have if you're doing brand marketing for a brand.
You know, you're like, hey, make sure, you know, if the brand is mentioned, make sure the TM is there, make sure the brand name is there, all that.
We were able to notice that it was not doing that.
Work, usually we use a cloud project to prototype.
And so we use a cloud project.

With the appropriate contextual documents for the client to create a post-processing list or editorial checklist, and then we're able to hand that to our pipeline and say, hey, I've created a set of instructions for you.
I would like you to deploy an agent to go out and perform these tasks on this body of copy that the previous step in the workflow has handed you.
Run that process, then evaluate your work.
We have a rubric for you.
Here it is.
Go check your work against this rubric.
Score yourself.
If you score less than 75% on any of these, go run the task again and evaluate yourself again.
Okay.
How'd you do?
Okay.
Go run it again.
Evaluate it again, please.
Oh, you scored well on everything.
You're above the threshold.
Wonderful.
Hand your work off to the next agent.
And then the next agent goes out and does its task.
Maybe it's like hyperlinking, right?
We need to make sure they...
Links are appropriate.
They're clinically approved sources, PubMed, Harvard Medical School, not johnsblog.com.
Saffron's the best.
Who cares, right?
So we have an agent that goes out and runs that task.

The reason I articulate this is because the problem or issue that we have right now is largely a matter of people who are intellectually curious enough to understand the agentic workflows, which are bleeding edge.
And then also the editorial skill set to be able to articulate instructions clearly and to engineer the context clearly so that those pipelines can then execute their tasks.
So I would say the biggest pain point, if you gathered everybody in the delivery org here and asked them together, what's your biggest pain point?
It is most likely time management.
It is the amount of time that it takes them to get a piece of copy ready for a client so that you can hand it off and say, hey, this is great.
You want to do your final checks and then we can go publish this thing.
want final checks get a so of of that you can copy
It's the time between that process happening and the time it exits the pipeline.
So once it comes out, how much work do I have to do before I can present it to the client?
Ideally, and in my experience, once the pipelines are properly adjusted, it's probably between 10 and 15 minutes when it's working right.

I can produce a clinically accurate, highly detailed, human-readable, germanely useful, genuinely useful article on a topic that has been reviewed by an OB, a naturopathic doctor, a lawyer, and a co-founder.
And they're like, this is great.
And I can do that with about five minutes worth of – or five to 10 minutes worth of human post-processing.

And that is like a complete brain-breaker for most marketing people because most of our clients, as you well know, because you're intimately familiar with how long it takes to create high-quality content, most of them are like –
They're to getting a couple of pieces a week out of a content team.
They're probably paying $100,000 a year, if not more, right?
Maybe two or three people, junior people.
Maybe it's like more like $250,000 because you're paying like a senior editor and an editor and then a couple of staff writers, maybe some freelancers.
Like they're used to paying a lot of money for this.
And they're used to being able to publish maybe a couple articles a week, you know?
And you mentioned 40 articles in a month.
Look how many freelancers it took to do all that, right?
And you know that all of those probably could have been better, right?
Like they were good, no denigration, but we just all know the time constraints, right?

So what if you had an insanely good deep researcher that will provide you with all the information you needed, factually accurate, verified and fact-checked information, raw material that you needed to write an article, and they could do it 15 times a day.
Like that alone, imagine the time savings, right?
Where you were like, hey, I have to write 40 how-to articles.
And I'm like, cool, I can have the deep research.
available to you today on all 40 of those articles, and then all that needs to happen is them, they need to write the articles, you probably would have been done within two weeks.

You know, if the research was all done and organized properly, and they were able to like write the copy clean and just be able to reference all the research that was right there at their fingertips, no Googling, no fact checking, no, you know, none of that.
So that compression of time is vital to the scalability of the company and our ability to execute like growth and continue to scale.

So our biggest pain point is how well are the MEs able to articulate what is going on with the pipelines to either improve them themselves by improving the inputs or context or file a ticket and say, hey, this thing's broken in this very specific way and here's why and here's how and here's how I think it could be improved.
And the engineering team goes like, okay, great, I'm going to take your editorial mumbo jumbo and translate that to code, right?
And like that basically is the.
is the pain point.
I would say the variance is probably like maybe 30% of our clients are being serviced in roughly that way.

Like people are just nailing it and they're getting most of their work done on Monday, frankly, and then spending the rest of the week like massaging and doing other tasks like monitoring, you know, analytics and things like that.
Um, and then there's a good chunk of the team that's like, I am underwater constantly and I need to figure out how to like improve these pipelines enough to get myself some breathing room.
And like, that's obviously nobody, nobody's alone out there, but it's just an, it's a fact of life.
